name: Content guide
description: The Content guide establishes the editorial and structural standards for creating web content within the Canonical design system. While the [Copy style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1AX-kSNztuAmShEoohe8L3LNLRnSKF7I0qkZGNeoGOok/edit?tab=t.0) addresses grammar, voice, and tone, this guide focuses on the strategic application and structural optimization of the page content.
content:
## **Composition model**

Effective content anatomy mirrors the user's mental model. It starts with a single, clear topic (the Page), structures a narrative journey (the Content layout), breaks that narrative into digestible chapters (Sections), and finally composes those chapters using specific building blocks.

**1\. Page - topic or concept**

The page is a complete, published document accessible under a unique URL. It represents a full piece of communication, a source for a specific concept or search intent.

*   **Purpose:** a page must have a clear purpose.
*   **Scope:** subject should be narrow enough to deliver a clear narrative journey to the end-user and broad enough to justify a unique URL.
*   **Identity:** every page is defined by a unique title (max 60 characters) and a meta description (max 160 characters) that establishes its context within the site.

**2\. Content layout  - narrative**

The layout defines the storytelling rhythm. It serves as the narrative flow that guides the user from the introduction to the conclusion (or conversion).

*   **Flow:** the order of content must always form a consistent narrative communicated with clear intent.
*   **Structure:** whether using a templated layout (for fixed needs like Case Studies) or a modular layout, the structure determines the hierarchy of information.
*   **Guidance:** layouts prioritize critical information at the top and maintain visual calmness to help users process information without clutter.

**3\. Sections  - chapters**

Sections are the cohesive units of the layout, each expressing an idea, concept or purpose.

*   **Separation:** sections break the narrative into logical steps (see available sections’ documentation for more information). Sections are visually separated by a horizontal rule to signal a change in topic to the user.
*   **Introduction:** every section must be introduced by a concise title (H2 or Heading Muted) that clearly defines the topic.

**4\. Blocks - grouping**

Blocks are repeatable micro-layouts that organize content within a section. They act as reusable compositions of UI elements.

*   **Organization:** blocks allow for parallel content units (such as a list of features, a grid of logos, or a set of pricing options) to be structured consistently.
*   **Composition:** a block is typically composed of a title (H3, H4, or H5) and associated primitives/components (text, image, link) that function together as a single group.
*   **Usage:** Blocks are used to present multiple related items side-by-side or in a sequence without breaking the flow of the main section topic.

**5\. UI artifacts - building elements**

Sites use UI artifacts from the Global and Sites tiers of the Canonical Design System to implement interface design. These artifacts are the fundamental building blocks used to populate sections and content blocks, guiding users through the narrative flow and enabling meaningful actions.

The most commonly used artifacts on Sites fall into three categories:

A) Textual

*   *   Headings: these act as signposts that outline the narrative structure. All headings must use a sentence case.
    *   Body Text: the primary vehicle for explanation or storytelling. Paragraphs must be concise, avoiding long, dense blocks of text. Sentences should average between 12 and 20 words.
    *   Microcopy: supporting text such as eyebrows (labels) or subtitles that provide context or categorization above or below headings. Should be short and precise.

B) Visual

*   *   Photos and illustrations: visuals used to support the narrative or explain complex topics. They must adhere to specific aspect ratios and be approved by the brand team or used directly from Canonical assets manager.
    *   Videos: time-based media used to demonstrate features or tell stories dynamically.
    *   Diagrams and logos: functional visuals for data visualization or brand association.

C) Interactive

*   *   CTAs (Call to Action): functional components (buttons) used to trigger an action. They must be clear, compelling, and aligned to the grid column of the content they relate to. They cannot be overused or compete for users' attention. Only one primary CTA should be visible at once to the user.
    *   Links: text elements directing navigation. Links must be descriptive (e.g., "Learn more about Ubuntu") rather than generic (e.g., "Read more").  
        

![Content guide.png](images/1389b977-b5e9-415c-a2d4-015272d7a5e6.png)

appliedRules.rule: Keep text concise. Favour brevity. Remove non-essential copy and avoid long, dense paragraphs.
Communicate with intent. Copy should either sell or explain. Avoid hybrid messaging that weakens both.

Avoid generic or empty statements. Do not publish vague, clichéd, or low-value claims (e.g. “cutting-edge”, “powerful”, “we are cloud”). Every statement must convey meaningful, differentiated information.
Show character and humanity. Maintain a professional yet approachable tone. Use clear, confident language that reflects expertise without being sterile or overly formal.
Follow the Copy style guide. For detailed rules of content execution, follow the official Canonical Copy style guide.
Enable a self-cleaning site. Monitor aged or outdated content and archive it proactively. Pages should expire and move out of the main IA automatically through process, metadata, or review cycles.
Avoid duplication. Do not rewrite or replicate existing information. Link to the canonical source instead.
One place per concept. Each topic should have a single authoritative page. Do not create new pages where updates to existing pages are appropriate.
Case studies are core sales assets. Present them with strong storytelling and design. They should feel aspirational, credible, and easy to explore.
Strengthen offers and calls to action. Ensure CTAs and lead-generation elements are compelling, clear, and optimised for conversion.
Use takeovers strategically. When implementing homepage or section takeovers, prioritise craft, wit, and visual quality. They should always lead somewhere meaningful.
Keep it lean. Design, copy, and IA should always move towards simplicity, reduction, and clarity.
Content layout should consist of minimum 3 and maximum 15 sections.
Only one primary CTA can be visible on the screen (with 100% zoom).
Number of CTAs in the layout should be as limited as possible.
Written English follows US spelling conventions.
The average sentence length in any paragraph should be between 12 and 20 words. Not longer.
Only illustrations from Canonical assets can be used for web pages (if illustration is missing, it can be requested from the brand team).
Only photos from our photo library (add link) can be used on web pages
Section must be introduced by a concise title that clearly defines the topic.
Headings/titles of sections and blocks can be linked if they follow links rules.
appliedRules.knownExceptionForConstructs: Hero, Data spotlight, Linked logo section, Logo section, Newsletter signup, Pricing block, Quote wrapper, Rich vertical list, Tiered list

appliedRules.rule: Use [webpage copy doc template](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EPA_Ea8ShIvyftAc9oVxZYUIMHfAPFF6S5x6FOvLkwM) to structure and visualize the content within the boundaries of available sections and layouts.
